text: During the urgent laparoscopy, the surgeon explores the abdominal cavity and identifies proximal jejunal loops fused together in the left upper quadrant.

During visceral lysis, a gangrenous jejunal loop is revealed, located twenty centimeters from the ligament of Treitz. The mesentery of the gangrenous loop appears retracted and thickened, likely due to mesenteric venous microthrombosis.

The surgeon uses a GIE stapler to section the proximal jejunum in a vital tissue area, located twenty centimeters from the ligament of Treitz. The mesentery is then sectioned using an ultrasonic dissector and secured with metal clips.

The surgeon carefully dissects the necrotic jejunal loop, ensuring no injury occurs to the adjacent transverse colon and its vascular structures. The mesentery is further sectioned using an ultrasonic dissector.

The surgeon releases the necrotic intestinal loop, measuring approximately twenty centimeters, and sections the distal part. Diastinal continuity is recreated using an antiperistaltic site-to-site intracorporeal anastomosis with a GIA stapler.

The surgeon closes the enterotomy with a double layer of T-zero absorbable running suture. A second running absorbable suture is applied to reinforce the proximal jejunal stump, and the anastomosis is covered with the omentum.
